name: Soonday (Korean Stuffed Sausage)
recipeInstructions:
In various cultures, cooks stuff the small intestine of cows, sheep, or pigs and boil, bake, or roast it. In Tunisia, this is called "merguez," in Eastern Europe as "kishke," in Indonesia, spectacularly with coconut milk as "usus." The Korean "soonday" differs significantly.
It originated in North Korea's cold mountain climate where wild mountain pig intestines were used. Today, it's prepared nationwide and sold at public markets in Pusan, Kyongu, Seoul, Taegu, and others. This sausage filling consists of rice, seasonings, and beef or pork blood (or substitute 8 ounces canned tomato puree).
1. Clean the intestine as received from your butcher once again. Rinse well in cold water, then soak in lightly salted water for 1 hour; this makes the intestine firm and easier to handle. Tie one end firmly with cotton string.
2. Prepare the stuffing by mixing cooked rice, garlic, ginger, salt, pepper, sesame oil, sesame seeds, scallions, and either blood or tomato puree. Loosely stuff the intestine using a machine or funnel, forcing the stuffing along its entire length. Do not fill too tightly as the intestine may split during cooking when the rice expands. Tie the open end firmly.
Alternatively, the intestine can be stuffed into individual sausages by cutting to desired lengths, tying, filling, and tying again.
3. Place the soonday in a large pan, curling it like a snail. Cover with lightly salted water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat to low and cook uncovered for 3/4 hour. At the end of this time, insert a skewer into the soonday to test for doneness. If the skewer is dry and the soonday firm to pressure, it is done. Cut diagonally into 1/4-inch thick slices and serve warm or at room temperature (warm is better). Serve on festive occasions, especially after rice, cabbage, or other harvests with your favorite Korean dip.
Serves 6 to 8.
